subject: Conditional Probability and Independence
question: Suppose 5 cards are drawn from a deck of 52 playing cards. What is the probability that all cards are the same suit, i.e. a flush?
explanation: We must find $\Pr(\text{a flush}) = \Pr(\text{5 spades}) + \Pr(\text{5 hearts}) + \Pr(\text{5 diamonds}) + \Pr(\text{5 clubs})$ Now, the probability of getting 5 spades is found as follows: $\Pr(\text{5 spades}) = \Pr(\text{1st card is a spade})\Pr(\text{2nd card is a spade} \mid \text{1st card is a spade}) \times \ldots \times \Pr(\text{5th card is a spade} \mid \text{1st,2nd,3rd,4th cards are spades}) = \frac{13}{52} \times \frac{12}{51} \times \frac{11}{50} \times \frac{10}{49} \times \frac{9}{48}$ Since the above calculation is the same for any of the four suits, $\Pr(\text{a flush}) = 4 \times \frac{13}{52} \times \frac{12}{51} \times \frac{11}{50} \times \frac{10}{49} \times \frac{9}{48}$ We end this section by showing that $\Pr(* \mid A)$ satisfies the properties of ordinary probabilities.
